stem: You are the consultation-liaison psychiatry registrar reviewing a 62-year-old woman in clinic. She has been on lithium 750 mg nocte for bipolar I disorder for 14 years and remains euthymic. Her last lithium level 4 months ago was 0.7 mmol/L. Her eGFR has declined gradually from 78 to 62 mL/min/1.73m^2 over 2 years. She is asymptomatic and her urine albumin to creatinine ratio is 2.1 mg/mmol. There is no NSAID use and her blood pressure is well controlled on perindopril 5 mg daily.

data_table.Examination: Euthymic affect; no tremor; no oedema; no goitre; abdomen soft
data_table.Investigations: Na 139, K 4.3, Cr 92, eGFR 62, TSH 3.8, fT4 14, corrected calcium 2.36, lithium 0.7 (target 0.6 to 0.8), HbA1c 5.4%
data_table.Medications: Lithium carbonate 750 mg nocte, perindopril 5 mg mane

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Continue lithium; recheck lithium level, U&E, TSH and calcium every 3 to 6 months with nephrology referral
options.rationale: Long-term lithium requires lithium level, U&E, TSH and calcium every 3 to 6 months once stable; eGFR below 60 warrants nephrology input for shared decision-making about continuation. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Mood Disorders)

options.text: Cease lithium and switch to sodium valproate; arrange psychiatry review within 2 weeks for cross-titration
options.rationale: Stable bipolar I on therapeutic lithium with stage 2 CKD does not mandate cessation; abrupt switch risks relapse and valproate carries its own monitoring burden. Continue with closer surveillance unless eGFR falls below 45 or declines by more than 4 mL/min/year. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Mood Disorders)

options.text: Reduce lithium to 375 mg nocte; recheck lithium level, U&E and TSH at 7 days then again at 4 weeks
options.rationale: Halving a therapeutic dose risks subtherapeutic levels and mood relapse without clear renal benefit; dose reduction is reserved for levels above the target range or symptomatic toxicity. (Source: Australian Medicines Handbook: Lithium)

options.text: Add candesartan 8 mg daily for renoprotection; recheck eGFR, electrolytes and lithium level in 4 weeks
options.rationale: Adding a second renin-angiotensin blocker is not indicated and ARB plus ACE inhibitor combination increases acute kidney injury and hyperkalaemia risk. (Source: KHA-CARI: CKD management)

options.text: Arrange an urgent renal tract ultrasound and refer to nephrology for renal biopsy within the next 14 days
options.rationale: Slow eGFR decline with minimal albuminuria does not meet criteria for urgent imaging or biopsy; lithium nephropathy is a clinical diagnosis with stable trajectory in most patients. (Source: KHA-CARI: CKD management)

explanation.summary: Long-term lithium therapy requires structured surveillance for renal, thyroid and parathyroid effects. Once a patient is stable, lithium level, U&E, TSH and corrected calcium are checked every 3 to 6 months. An eGFR below 60 mL/min/1.73m^2 warrants nephrology input to weigh ongoing lithium against relapse risk. Abrupt cessation or aggressive dose reduction is reserved for clear toxicity or rapid renal decline.
key_points: Routine lithium monitoring is lithium level, U&E, TSH and calcium every 3 to 6 months when stable
Refer to nephrology when eGFR is below 60 or declines rapidly
Avoid combined ACE inhibitor plus ARB therapy
Lithium nephropathy is usually a clinical diagnosis without biopsy
explanation.pearls: Nephrology referral threshold: eGFR below 60 mL/min/1.73m^2 OR decline greater than 4 mL/min per year.

subtopic_detail: Lithium-induced hypothyroidism in early pregnancy planning
stem: You are the psychiatry registrar reviewing a 31-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder, stable on lithium 900 mg nocte for 5 years. She and her partner are planning pregnancy in 6 months. She has gained 3 kg, feels cold, and reports new constipation over 8 weeks. Her last manic episode required ICU admission and her psychiatrist regards her as high relapse risk. She is using condoms reliably. The pre-pregnancy bloods are back.

data_table.Examination: Mildly dry skin; slow ankle reflex relaxation; no goitre; affect euthymic; no tremor
data_table.Investigations: TSH 8.4, fT4 11 (low-normal), TPO antibody positive, lithium 0.8, Cr 76, eGFR 92, beta-hCG negative
data_table.Medications: Lithium carbonate 900 mg nocte; folic acid 500 micrograms daily

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Cease lithium immediately and substitute quetiapine 300 mg nocte; refer to obstetric psychiatry for monitoring
options.rationale: Sudden lithium cessation in a high relapse risk patient is associated with manic recurrence; lithium nephrotoxicity is not the issue here and hypothyroidism is correctable. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Mood Disorders)

options.text: Continue lithium at current dose and commence levothyroxine 50 micrograms daily; recheck TSH and fT4 in 6 weeks
options.rationale: Lithium-induced hypothyroidism is managed with thyroxine replacement rather than lithium withdrawal in a stable bipolar patient at high relapse risk; pregnancy planning increases the imperative for euthyroid status before conception. (Source: AMH: Levothyroxine)

options.text: Continue lithium and start carbimazole 5 mg daily; recheck thyroid function and lithium level in 4 weeks
options.rationale: Carbimazole is used for hyperthyroidism; the patient has biochemical hypothyroidism and thyroxine is required. (Source: AMH: Thyroid disorders)

options.text: Reduce lithium to 600 mg nocte and start levothyroxine 25 micrograms daily; recheck TSH and lithium in 2 weeks
options.rationale: Reducing lithium in a high relapse risk patient risks decompensation; subtherapeutic dosing is not justified by hypothyroidism which is independently treated. (Source: AMH: Lithium)

options.text: Continue lithium, withhold levothyroxine and recheck TSH in 12 weeks before any decision on thyroid replacement
options.rationale: Delay risks worsening hypothyroidism through pregnancy planning and conception; TSH above 4 in a woman planning pregnancy with positive TPO antibody warrants treatment now. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology)

explanation.summary: Lithium-induced hypothyroidism affects up to 1 in 5 patients on long-term therapy and is more common in women, especially with positive TPO antibodies. The standard approach is to treat with levothyroxine rather than to discontinue lithium, particularly in patients at high relapse risk. In a woman planning pregnancy, achieving euthyroid status before conception reduces fetal neurodevelopmental risk. Lithium itself is teratogenic but the absolute Ebstein anomaly risk is lower than historically taught and continuation may be appropriate with shared decision-making.
key_points: Lithium hypothyroidism is treated with levothyroxine, not by stopping lithium
Target TSH below 2.5 mIU/L preconception and in first trimester
TPO antibody positivity predicts lithium hypothyroidism
High relapse risk bipolar warrants continuation through pregnancy planning
explanation.pearls: Levothyroxine 50 micrograms daily is a reasonable starting dose in non-pregnant adults; titrate by TSH at 6 weeks.

stem: You are the early psychosis registrar reviewing a 24-year-old man who started olanzapine 10 mg nocte 3 months ago after a first episode of schizophrenia. He reports a 4 kg weight gain and increased appetite. He smokes 10 cigarettes daily, drinks no alcohol, and has a strong family history of type 2 diabetes. He is otherwise asymptomatic, attending TAFE, and adherent to treatment.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 124/78, HR 78, RR 14, SpO2 99% RA, T 36.7; waist 96 cm, BMI 27
data_table.Examination: Settled affect; no EPS; no tremor; no acanthosis nigricans
data_table.Investigations: Fasting glucose 5.5, HbA1c 5.8%, total cholesterol 5.2, LDL 3.4, HDL 1.0, triglycerides 2.0, ALT 32, prolactin 280 (normal)

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management strategy?

options.text: Continue olanzapine; arrange lifestyle counselling and recheck weight, fasting glucose and lipids at 6 months
options.rationale: While lifestyle counselling is correct, a single recheck at 6 months delays detection of rapid metabolic decline during the first year on a high-risk antipsychotic. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Schizophrenia)

options.text: Switch to aripiprazole 10 mg daily over 4 weeks; recheck weight, fasting glucose and lipids in 3 months
options.rationale: Premature switching after only 3 months of effective treatment risks relapse; metabolic side effects should first be addressed with lifestyle and surveillance, with switch reserved for established intolerance or inefficacy. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Schizophrenia)

options.text: Continue olanzapine with lifestyle intervention; arrange 3-monthly weight, BP, fasting glucose and lipids in year 1
options.rationale: First year on a metabolically active antipsychotic requires 3-monthly metabolic monitoring including weight, waist circumference, BP, fasting glucose and lipids; lifestyle intervention is first line for weight gain at this stage. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Schizophrenia)

options.text: Continue olanzapine; add metformin 500 mg twice daily and recheck weight, HbA1c and fasting lipids in 3 months
options.rationale: Metformin for antipsychotic weight gain is considered when lifestyle measures fail and after at least 3 to 6 months; HbA1c 5.8% does not meet diabetes criteria and lifestyle is the first step. (Source: AMH: Metformin)

options.text: Stop olanzapine; switch to risperidone 2 mg twice daily and commence atorvastatin 20 mg nocte for CV risk
options.rationale: Risperidone is also metabolically active and may worsen prolactin; statin therapy is not indicated by current lipid profile or absolute cardiovascular risk in a 24 year old. (Source: AMH: Antipsychotics)

explanation.summary: Second-generation antipsychotic metabolic monitoring follows a structured schedule, especially in the first year. Weight, waist, blood pressure, fasting glucose and lipids are checked at baseline, 3 months, then 3 to 6 monthly. Olanzapine carries the highest metabolic burden alongside clozapine. Lifestyle intervention is first line for weight gain before adjunct metformin or antipsychotic switch.
key_points: Baseline, 3 month then 3 to 6 monthly metabolic monitoring during the first year
Olanzapine and clozapine carry the highest metabolic risk
Metformin is second line for antipsychotic weight gain after lifestyle fails
Switch to a lower metabolic burden agent is reserved for established harm
explanation.pearls: Metformin can be added if weight gain exceeds 5% over 3 months despite lifestyle and the patient is otherwise well controlled on antipsychotic.

stem: You are the psychiatry registrar reviewing a 28-year-old woman with schizophrenia, stable on risperidone 4 mg daily for 18 months. She reports 4 months of amenorrhoea, breast tenderness and recent galactorrhoea. She is not pregnant. She is otherwise mentally well and has previously failed olanzapine due to weight gain. Her prolactin is 1850 mIU/L (normal less than 500 in non-pregnant adult).

data_table.Examination: Galactorrhoea bilateral on expression; no visual field defect; no acromegalic features
data_table.Investigations: Prolactin 1850, beta-hCG negative, TSH 1.8, fT4 14, oestradiol low, LH and FSH low-normal; MRI pituitary normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Continue risperidone and commence cabergoline 0.25 mg twice weekly; recheck prolactin in 6 weeks
options.rationale: Dopamine agonists antagonise antipsychotic action and risk psychotic relapse; they are not first line for antipsychotic-induced hyperprolactinaemia. (Source: AMH: Cabergoline)

options.text: Reduce risperidone to 2 mg daily; arrange endocrinology review and recheck prolactin within 4 weeks
options.rationale: Dose reduction in a stable patient risks relapse and rarely normalises prolactin from this range; switch to a prolactin-sparing agent is preferred. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Schizophrenia)

options.text: Continue risperidone at current dose; reassure that mild prolactin rise is expected and recheck symptoms in 6 months
options.rationale: Symptomatic hyperprolactinaemia with amenorrhoea and galactorrhoea is not acceptable to leave untreated due to bone density loss and quality of life impact. (Source: eTG: Endocrinology)

options.text: Switch risperidone to aripiprazole 10 mg daily over 4 weeks; recheck prolactin and review symptoms at 6 weeks
options.rationale: Aripiprazole is prolactin-sparing and often partially reverses antipsychotic-induced hyperprolactinaemia; switch is preferred over add-on cabergoline. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Schizophrenia)

options.text: Cease risperidone abruptly; commence quetiapine 100 mg twice daily and refer to gynaecology for amenorrhoea workup
options.rationale: Abrupt antipsychotic cessation risks rebound and psychotic relapse; quetiapine is reasonable but cross-titration is needed, and gynaecology referral is unnecessary if antipsychotic switch resolves the prolactin. (Source: AMH: Antipsychotics)

explanation.summary: Symptomatic hyperprolactinaemia from risperidone, paliperidone and amisulpride is managed by switching to a prolactin-sparing agent such as aripiprazole, quetiapine or olanzapine. Aripiprazole has the most evidence for prolactin reduction without antipsychotic withdrawal. Dopamine agonists are avoided where possible because they may precipitate psychotic relapse. Persistent symptomatic hyperprolactinaemia causes bone density loss in both sexes.
key_points: Risperidone, paliperidone and amisulpride are high-prolactin agents
Aripiprazole is the prolactin-sparing switch of choice
Dopamine agonists risk psychotic relapse
Long-term hyperprolactinaemia causes osteoporosis
explanation.pearls: Adjunctive aripiprazole 5 to 10 mg added to risperidone is an alternative when switching is not possible; partial dopamine agonism normalises prolactin in many patients.

stem: You are the adult psychiatry registrar reviewing a 26-year-old man with newly diagnosed ADHD-combined type confirmed on DIVA-5 interview and corroborated by partner and parents. Childhood symptoms documented in school reports. He has no current substance use disorder, no cardiac history, no family history of sudden cardiac death and is otherwise well. He drives a car as part of his work delivering parcels. His ECG and BP are normal.

data_table.Examination: Distractible during interview; otherwise normal; ECG sinus rhythm QTc 410 ms
data_table.Investigations: Urine drug screen negative; TSH normal; FBC, U&E and LFTs normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line pharmacological choice and the most important counselling point about driving?

options.text: Commence atomoxetine 40 mg daily; advise that he must surrender his licence to Vicroads while on stimulant medication
options.rationale: Atomoxetine is second line for adults and licence surrender is not required for legally prescribed stimulants. (Source: Austroads: Assessing Fitness to Drive)

options.text: Commence guanfacine extended release 1 mg daily; advise that driving is contraindicated for the first 6 weeks of treatment
options.rationale: Guanfacine is third line for adult ADHD and there is no 6-week driving ban; only sedation while titrating should be considered. (Source: AMH: Guanfacine)

options.text: Commence bupropion 150 mg daily; advise that ADHD without comorbid depression rarely requires pharmacotherapy
options.rationale: Bupropion is not approved for adult ADHD in Australia and the premise that ADHD rarely requires medication is incorrect. (Source: AMH: Bupropion)

options.text: Commence dexamfetamine 5 mg twice daily; counsel that he must inform police about stimulant use at every interaction
options.rationale: Dexamfetamine is a reasonable agent but the advice on police disclosure is incorrect; patients only need to comply with general driving licence and medical reporting rules. (Source: Austroads: Assessing Fitness to Drive)

options.text: Commence lisdexamfetamine 30 mg morning; counsel that untreated ADHD increases crash risk and treated driving is generally safe
options.rationale: Lisdexamfetamine is first line for adult ADHD per RANZCP guidance and Austroads recognises that treated ADHD is associated with lower crash risk than untreated ADHD. (Source: RANZCP CPG: ADHD)

explanation.summary: First-line pharmacotherapy for adult ADHD in Australia is a long-acting stimulant, with lisdexamfetamine or methylphenidate as preferred options. ECG and cardiovascular history are checked before initiation but routine cardiac imaging is not required without risk factors. Austroads guidance supports driving on stimulants when symptoms are controlled and there is no impairment, because treated ADHD has lower crash rates than untreated. Atomoxetine, guanfacine and bupropion are non-stimulant options reserved for stimulant failure, substance misuse risk or contraindications.
key_points: Lisdexamfetamine and methylphenidate are first-line for adult ADHD
ECG and BP at baseline; no routine cardiac imaging without risk factors
Treated ADHD has lower crash risk than untreated
Atomoxetine is second line
explanation.pearls: Lisdexamfetamine starting dose 30 mg morning; titrate weekly to 50 to 70 mg based on response and tolerability.

stem: You are the GP registrar reviewing a 48-year-old man with COPD who smokes 30 cigarettes per day. He has tried nicotine patches alone twice unsuccessfully and wants to quit before a planned hip replacement in 12 weeks. He has stable schizophrenia on aripiprazole, no recent self-harm, no current depressive symptoms and no recent suicidal ideation. He is keen to try something more effective.

data_table.Examination: Mood euthymic; affect reactive; no thought disorder; no suicidal ideation
data_table.Investigations: FBC, U&E, LFTs normal; PHQ-9 score 4 (minimal); spirometry FEV1 62% predicted
data_table.Medications: Aripiprazole 10 mg daily; tiotropium inhaler; salbutamol PRN

lead_in: Which pharmacotherapy is most appropriate for this patient?

options.text: Varenicline 0.5 mg daily for 3 days, then 0.5 mg twice daily for 4 days, then 1 mg twice daily; quit day at 8 to 14 days
options.rationale: Varenicline is the most effective single agent for smoking cessation and is appropriate in stable schizophrenia without active suicidality; modern guidance supports use with monitoring rather than avoidance. (Source: RACGP Smoking Cessation Guidelines)

options.text: Bupropion 150 mg daily for 6 days then 150 mg twice daily for 12 weeks; quit day at 7 to 14 days from start
options.rationale: Bupropion is reasonable but less effective than varenicline and lowers seizure threshold; in schizophrenia varenicline now has the best evidence base. (Source: RACGP Smoking Cessation Guidelines)

options.text: Nicotine replacement patch 21 mg daily with 4 mg gum PRN for breakthrough cravings for 12 weeks total
options.rationale: Combination NRT is reasonable but the patient has already failed two NRT-based attempts; escalation to varenicline is appropriate. (Source: RACGP Smoking Cessation Guidelines)

options.text: Nortriptyline 25 mg nocte titrated to 75 mg nocte over 4 weeks; quit day at 14 to 21 days from start
options.rationale: Nortriptyline is fourth line and not appropriate when varenicline is available and tolerated; antidepressant effect is unwanted here. (Source: RACGP Smoking Cessation Guidelines)

options.text: Clonidine 50 micrograms twice daily titrated to 150 micrograms twice daily; quit day at 7 to 10 days from start
options.rationale: Clonidine is rarely used due to side effects and is reserved for failure of first and second line agents; it offers no advantage over varenicline. (Source: AMH: Clonidine)

explanation.summary: Varenicline is the most effective single agent for smoking cessation with approximately a doubling of long-term quit rates compared with placebo. Previous neuropsychiatric safety concerns have been substantially refuted by the EAGLES trial, and varenicline can be used in stable schizophrenia and depression with appropriate monitoring. Combination NRT or escalation to varenicline is recommended after monotherapy NRT failure. Quit day is typically set 8 to 14 days after starting varenicline to allow steady state.
key_points: Varenicline is first line for high-dependence smokers and after NRT failure
EAGLES trial supports use in stable mental illness with monitoring
Quit day 8 to 14 days after varenicline initiation
Bupropion is second line and lowers seizure threshold
explanation.pearls: Smoking is a CYP1A2 inducer; cessation can raise clozapine and olanzapine levels by 30 to 50 percent, requiring dose adjustment.

stem: You are the emergency medicine registrar called to a 32-year-old man in the resus bay who arrived via police. He is shouting, swinging at staff and security, and refusing oral medication. There is no obtainable history beyond that he was found wandering on a freeway. Five staff and two security officers have established physical containment in lateral recumbent position. There is no IV access. His airway is patent.

data_table.Vital signs: BP estimated 150/90 by manual, HR 132, RR 28, SpO2 unmeasured, T not yet measured; finger-prick glucose 6.8
data_table.Examination: Sweating, dilated pupils, dry mucosa, no obvious trauma, no track marks visible
data_table.Investigations: ECG not yet feasible; capillary glucose 6.8; venous gas pending

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first parenteral medication?

options.text: Midazolam 10 mg intramuscular into deltoid; reassess at 15 minutes and repeat 5 mg if not adequately settled
options.rationale: Midazolam alone is associated with greater respiratory depression than droperidol in this setting and is second line in current Australian ED practice. (Source: ACEM/ACEP Guideline: Acute Behavioural Disturbance)

options.text: Droperidol 10 mg intramuscular into deltoid; reassess at 15 minutes and repeat once if not adequately settled
options.rationale: Droperidol 10 mg IM is first-line for severe acute behavioural disturbance in Australian emergency practice; onset 10 to 20 minutes and good safety profile in agitated patients without known long QT. (Source: ACEM/ACEP Guideline: Acute Behavioural Disturbance)

options.text: Haloperidol 5 mg with lorazepam 2 mg intramuscular into deltoid; reassess at 30 minutes for response
options.rationale: The haloperidol plus lorazepam combination is widely used internationally but has slower onset than droperidol monotherapy; current Australian guidance preferences droperidol. (Source: ACEM/ACEP Guideline: Acute Behavioural Disturbance)

options.text: Olanzapine 10 mg intramuscular into deltoid; await onset over 20 minutes before considering further sedation
options.rationale: Olanzapine IM is an option but reserved when droperidol is contraindicated; combined olanzapine plus benzodiazepine within 1 hour increases hypotension and oversedation risk. (Source: AMH: Olanzapine IM)

options.text: Ketamine 4 mg/kg intramuscular into deltoid as immediate dissociative sedation pending ICU transfer for intubation
options.rationale: Ketamine is reserved for failure of first and second line sedation; routine first-use ketamine in an undifferentiated patient is not standard and risks laryngospasm without airway preparation. (Source: ACEM/ACEP Guideline: Acute Behavioural Disturbance)

explanation.summary: Droperidol 10 mg IM is the first-line parenteral sedative for severe acute behavioural disturbance in Australian emergency departments based on the DORM and DORM II studies. It offers fast onset, predictable duration and a favourable safety profile when QT prolongation risk factors are absent. Second-line options include olanzapine IM or midazolam IM, and ketamine is reserved for refractory cases. Continuous monitoring of airway, oxygenation and circulation begins as soon as feasible after sedation.
key_points: Droperidol 10 mg IM is first line for severe ABD in Australian EDs
Onset 10 to 20 minutes, repeat in 15 minutes if needed
Olanzapine IM avoided within 1 hour of benzodiazepines
Ketamine reserved for failure of standard sedation
explanation.pearls: Monitor for respiratory depression and check ECG once safe; QTc above 500 ms or known long QT favours olanzapine over droperidol.

stem: You are the emergency medicine consultant overseeing the resuscitation of a 26-year-old man with suspected methamphetamine intoxication. He has received droperidol 10 mg IM 25 minutes ago and a repeat droperidol 10 mg IM 10 minutes ago. He continues to thrash against restraints despite a brief period of partial settling. His core temperature is now 39.1, HR 168 sinus on monitor, BP 168/104, and he has bitten his lip with blood in airway. There is one functional IV line. The team is preparing to intubate.

data_table.Vital signs: T 39.1, HR 168 sinus, BP 168/104, RR 36, SpO2 92% on 15 L NRB; capillary glucose 8.2
data_table.Examination: Diaphoretic, rigid, clenched jaw, lip laceration with airway blood; pupils 6 mm reactive
data_table.Investigations: VBG pH 7.18, lactate 8.2, K 5.8, Na 142; CK 4200; troponin pending; ECG sinus tachycardia, QTc 480
data_table.Medications: Droperidol 10 mg IM x2 in last 25 minutes

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Ketamine 4 mg/kg intramuscular as immediate sedation; defer intubation until obtainable cooperation for nasal airway placement
options.rationale: IM ketamine in this patient is acceptable as a rescue strategy but defers definitive airway control in a patient with active aspiration and severe acidaemia; the team is already preparing intubation, and IV access exists. (Source: ACEM/ACEP Guideline: Acute Behavioural Disturbance)

options.text: Repeat droperidol 10 mg IM and add midazolam 5 mg IV; reassess in 10 minutes before considering airway intervention
options.rationale: Third dose of droperidol with benzodiazepine in an unstable patient with airway compromise wastes time and risks dropping BP without addressing temperature; intubation is required. (Source: ACEM/ACEP Guideline: Acute Behavioural Disturbance)

options.text: Rapid sequence intubation with ketamine 1.5 mg/kg IV and rocuronium 1.2 mg/kg IV; commence active cooling with cold IV fluids
options.rationale: Sympathomimetic toxidrome with hyperthermia above 39, rhabdomyolysis, acidaemia and airway compromise meets criteria for definitive airway control; ketamine RSI avoids sympathetic surge from suxamethonium and active cooling addresses the principal driver of mortality. (Source: ACEM/ACEP Guideline: Acute Behavioural Disturbance)

options.text: Dantrolene 1 mg/kg IV with active surface cooling; transfer to ICU and defer airway intervention
options.rationale: Dantrolene is for malignant hyperthermia and NMS, not sympathomimetic hyperthermia which responds to sedation, paralysis and external cooling; airway must be secured first. (Source: AMH: Dantrolene)

options.text: Suxamethonium 1.5 mg/kg IV alone with bag mask ventilation; insert nasogastric tube for activated charcoal administration
options.rationale: Suxamethonium without sedation is inhumane; further hyperkalaemia in rhabdomyolysis is dangerous; charcoal is not indicated for sympathomimetic intoxication. (Source: AMH: Suxamethonium)

explanation.summary: Severe sympathomimetic toxidrome with hyperthermia above 39, severe lactic acidaemia, rhabdomyolysis and airway compromise mandates definitive airway control rather than further procedural sedation. Ketamine RSI is preferred over suxamethonium because suxamethonium can exacerbate hyperkalaemia in rhabdomyolysis and induces sympathetic surge. Active cooling with cold IV fluids and exposure is the principal disease-modifying intervention; hyperthermia drives multi-organ failure. Dantrolene is for malignant hyperthermia and NMS, not psychostimulant toxicity.
key_points: Hyperthermia above 39 in sympathomimetic toxidrome warrants immediate cooling and airway control
Ketamine RSI preferred over suxamethonium with rhabdomyolysis and hyperkalaemia
Dantrolene has no role in psychostimulant hyperthermia
Repeated sedation without airway control wastes resuscitation time
explanation.pearls: Cold IV crystalloid at 30 mL/kg over 30 minutes plus surface cooling can drop temperature by 1 degree per 30 minutes; target below 38.5 within 1 hour.

stem: You are the consultation-liaison psychiatry registrar reviewing a 28-year-old woman admitted under medicine for dehydration and reduced oral intake over 5 days. She has known schizophrenia previously stable on aripiprazole. She is mute, holds bizarre postures for minutes at a time, and appears to be staring at the wall. Bush-Francis catatonia rating scale is 14. CT brain unremarkable. EEG, infection screen and lithium not contributory. Her aripiprazole was stopped on admission by the medical team.

data_table.Examination: Mute, waxy flexibility, posturing arms above head 60 seconds, no tremor, no rigidity, no fever
data_table.Investigations: CK 320 (mildly elevated), Na 142, K 4.0, WCC 8.4, CRP 6, TSH normal, CT brain normal, lumbar puncture deferred
data_table.Medications: Aripiprazole ceased on admission; IV maintenance fluids

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Restart aripiprazole 10 mg orally daily; continue intravenous maintenance fluids and reassess catatonia at 48 hours
options.rationale: Restarting an antipsychotic without first addressing catatonia risks precipitating neuroleptic malignant syndrome; lorazepam is required first. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Catatonia)

options.text: Administer haloperidol 5 mg intramuscularly with promethazine 25 mg intramuscularly and reassess in 30 minutes
options.rationale: Antipsychotics can worsen catatonia and precipitate NMS, especially in malignant catatonia; first-generation high-potency agents are particularly avoided. (Source: AMH: Haloperidol)

options.text: Refer urgently for electroconvulsive therapy without prior pharmacological challenge; consent through guardianship board
options.rationale: ECT is second line after lorazepam fails, although highly effective in malignant catatonia; a trial of lorazepam should normally precede ECT. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Catatonia)

options.text: Administer lorazepam 2 mg intravenously and reassess Bush-Francis score within 10 to 15 minutes for response
options.rationale: Lorazepam challenge with 1 to 2 mg IV is the diagnostic and initial therapeutic step for catatonia; a 50 percent reduction in Bush-Francis score within 10 to 15 minutes is supportive and guides ongoing scheduled lorazepam. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Catatonia)

options.text: Administer amantadine 100 mg orally twice daily; arrange MRI brain with contrast over the next 48 hours
options.rationale: Amantadine is third line after lorazepam and ECT; structural imaging has already been done and contrast is not indicated for catatonia workup at this stage. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Catatonia)

explanation.summary: Catatonia is diagnosed clinically using Bush-Francis criteria and confirmed by a lorazepam challenge. Lorazepam 1 to 2 mg IV given over a few minutes with reassessment at 10 to 15 minutes is both diagnostic and the first treatment step. A clear response supports scheduled lorazepam 6 to 24 mg daily titrated to effect. Antipsychotics, especially high-potency first generation agents, can worsen catatonia and precipitate malignant catatonia or NMS and should be withheld until catatonia resolves. ECT is highly effective second-line treatment.
key_points: Bush-Francis is the standard catatonia rating tool
Lorazepam 1 to 2 mg IV is both diagnostic and therapeutic
Avoid antipsychotics during active catatonia
ECT is second line and definitive treatment for refractory cases
explanation.pearls: Scheduled lorazepam doses of 6 to 24 mg per day are often tolerated without sedation when catatonia is active because of altered GABA tone.

stem: You are the psychiatry registrar reviewing a 22-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder. She has been stable on sodium valproate 1000 mg daily for 3 years. She has a new male partner and reports inconsistent condom use. She does not currently want to become pregnant but is open to pregnancy in 2 to 3 years. She has not been counselled about valproate and pregnancy. Her TGA-listed indication and the AU pregnancy prevention requirements apply.

data_table.Examination: Euthymic, no tremor, no peripheral oedema, normal gait
data_table.Investigations: FBC normal, LFTs normal, valproate 480 micromol/L (target 350 to 700), beta-hCG negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue valproate; arrange annual ultrasound screening for ovarian morphology and reassess teratogenic counselling in 12 months
options.rationale: Polycystic ovary morphology is a recognised valproate effect but routine annual ultrasound surveillance is not standard; teratogenic counselling cannot wait. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Mood Disorders)

options.text: Switch valproate to lamotrigine 200 mg daily; commence folic acid 5 mg daily and continue current condom-only contraception
options.rationale: Premature switch may destabilise bipolar; condom-only contraception is not reliable enough for a teratogenic medication; counselling and effective contraception should come first. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Mood Disorders)

options.text: Continue valproate; commence high-dose folic acid 5 mg daily and reassure that contraception is not yet necessary
options.rationale: Folic acid does not mitigate valproate teratogenicity meaningfully and effective contraception is required during valproate exposure regardless of pregnancy plans. (Source: TGA Valproate Pregnancy Prevention)

options.text: Cease valproate abruptly; bridge with quetiapine 300 mg nocte and arrange psychiatry review within 2 weeks for stability
options.rationale: Abrupt cessation risks relapse and quetiapine is not equivalent to valproate for bipolar maintenance; managed switch is appropriate but only after counselling. (Source: AMH: Sodium valproate)

options.text: Continue valproate; arrange counselling on teratogenic risk and ensure highly effective contraception is in place
options.rationale: Valproate carries up to 10 percent major congenital malformation risk and 30 to 40 percent neurodevelopmental risk; Australian guidance mandates documented teratogenic counselling and reliable contraception for women of reproductive potential, with consideration of switch when planning pregnancy. (Source: RANZCP CPG: Mood Disorders)

explanation.summary: Valproate is the most teratogenic anticonvulsant and is associated with major malformations in about 10 percent of exposed pregnancies and neurodevelopmental impairment in up to 40 percent. The TGA in 2023 introduced Pregnancy Prevention Program requirements for valproate, including documented annual counselling, effective contraception and acknowledgement forms for women of reproductive potential. Switching is considered when pregnancy is planned but must be done in a stable patient with bridging therapy. Lamotrigine and lithium are commonly used alternatives.
key_points: Valproate has 10 percent malformation risk and 30 to 40 percent neurodevelopmental risk
TGA Pregnancy Prevention Program mandates annual documented counselling and contraception
Folic acid does not negate valproate teratogenicity
Switch to lamotrigine or lithium when planning pregnancy in stable bipolar
explanation.pearls: Long-acting reversible contraception (LARC) such as etonogestrel implant or copper IUD is preferred for women on valproate due to user-independent reliability.
